steps.prompt: Drag the corners and watch the angles
steps.subtitle: Any other triangle with these same three angles is similar to this one — same shape, any size.

graphic.name: photo-enlarge
graphic.caption: Enlarge a 4×6 photo to 8×12 and it still looks right — same shape, just bigger. That's similarity.

steps.body:
Two triangles are similar when they have the same shape — the same three angles — even if one is bigger.
The bigger one is just a scaled copy of the smaller. Here an [original triangle](blue) and a [scaled copy](orange).
You'll discover the trap that catches almost everyone: the sides and the area do NOT grow by the same amount.

steps.prompt: Two triangles each have a 50° angle and a 60° angle. Same shape?
steps.subtitle: Same shape means similar — you don't need to know any side lengths.

options.label: Yes — if two angles match, the third must match too, so all three agree.
Only if they are the same size.
options.feedback: Similar means same shape, not same size. Equal angles are all you need.

options.label: Not unless we know a side.
options.feedback: Two equal angles are enough: the third is 180° − 50° − 60° = 70° in both triangles.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Two matching angles — the AA test — force the third to match, so the triangles are similar.
feedback.incorrect: If two angles match, the third does too (all three add to 180°). That's the AA test.

steps.prompt: AA similarity, and the k² surprise
steps.body:
AA test: if two angles of one triangle match two angles of another, the triangles are similar.
Then every pair of matching sides shares one [scale factor k](magenta), but the [area](green) scales by [k](magenta)².
Double the sides ([k](magenta) = 2) and the [area](green) becomes 2² = 4 times as big — not 2. That's the whole trick.

steps.prompt: Match each length scale to its area scale
steps.subtitle: Lengths scale by k; areas scale by k². Tap a length scale, then its area scale.
steps.doneNote: Area always grows by k² — the square of the length factor.

steps.subtitle: Lengths scale by [k](magenta); area scales by [k](magenta)². Judge each claim.

cards.statement: If two angles of one triangle match two angles of another, the triangles are similar.
cards.note: The AA test — the third angle is forced to match (all three add to 180°).

cards.statement: Scaling every side by k = 2 doubles the area.
cards.note: No — area scales by k² = 2² = 4, so it quadruples. Picture a 1×1 square becoming a 2×2 square.

cards.statement: If corresponding sides are in the ratio 3 : 1, the areas are in the ratio 9 : 1.
cards.note: Area scales by the square of the side ratio: 3² = 9.

cards.statement: Triangles must be the same size to be similar.
cards.note: Similar means same shape, not same size — equal angles are all you need.

steps.doneNote: Equal angles ⇒ similar; lengths scale by k, but area scales by k².

steps.subtitle: A student enlarges a photo and reasons about its area. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: The photo is enlarged so every side is 3x longer (k = 3).
Its width and its height each get 3x bigger.
So the area also gets 3x bigger.
The big photo's area is 3x the small one.

steps.explanation: Area scales by k^2, not k. With k = 3 the area grows 3^2 = 9 times. A 1x1 square scaled by 3 becomes a 3x3 grid of 9 little squares.
steps.hint: Lengths scale by k, but area scales by k squared. What is 3 squared?
steps.doneNote: Right — area scales by k² = 9, not 3.

steps.prompt: Scale the photo, then its area
steps.intro: A [4×6 photo](blue) is enlarged to an [8×12 copy](orange). Find the [scale factor k](magenta), the missing side, then how many times bigger the [area](green) gets — one move at a time.

parts.prompt: The 4-unit side becomes 8. What is the scale factor k = 8 ÷ 4?
parts.hint: Divide the new length by the old: 8 ÷ 4.
parts.note: k = 2 — every length is multiplied by 2.

parts.prompt: The other side is 6. Scaled by k = 2, what does it become?
parts.hint: Multiply the side by the scale factor: 6 × 2.

parts.prompt: How many times bigger is the copy's area? (Find k² with k = 2.)
parts.hint: Area scales by the square of the length factor: 2².
parts.note: k² = 2² = 4 — the area is 4 times as big, not 2.

steps.doneNote: Lengths scale by k = 2, but area scales by k² = 4. That's the whole trick.
